title: Today's Tech Brief: The Pope, AI, and a Dash of Irony
summary: Exploring the intersection of technology and faith, as the Pope's recent encyclical on AI raises interesting questions about authorship and innovation.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI?
imageCaption: Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI? (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm diving into the latest tech news, and a story that caught my eye involves the Pope and artificial intelligence. It seems parts of the Pope's recent encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas, may have been written with the help of AI. Yes, you read that right - the Pope might have used AI to write about the dangers of AI. It's a bit like using a fire extinguisher to put out a fire, only to find out the extinguisher was made of flammable materials.

According to reports from The Verge, analyses suggest that certain sections of the encyclical do indeed appear to have been authored by AI. This raises a multitude of questions about the role of technology in religious discourse and the potential implications for how we perceive and interact with religious texts. It's also worth noting that the Vatican invited Christopher Olah, co-founder of Anthropic, to speak at the presentation of the encyclical, signaling a unique alliance between the Catholic Church and Silicon Valley.

Wired has an interesting piece on what the Pope's encyclical says about the power of AI. Essentially, the Pope is highlighting how AI is not just another technology, but a force that is deeply intertwined with our daily lives, influencing what we see, read, and even how we work. It's a nuanced view that recognizes both the potential benefits and the dangers of relying too heavily on AI. I find it fascinating that the Pope is using this platform to encourage a broader conversation about the impact of technology on society.

The partnership between the Vatican and Anthropic is also noteworthy. It shows that even in the most traditional of institutions, there's a recognition of the importance of engaging with modern technology. This isn't just about the Pope using AI to write an encyclical; it's about the Church acknowledging that technology is a fundamental part of our world and that it needs to be part of the conversation. What stands out to me is how this move could pave the way for more collaborations between tech and religious or traditional institutions, leading to some very interesting and potentially groundbreaking discussions.

Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI?, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: AI News Did the Pope use AI to write about the dangers of AI? Analyses have determined that parts of the Magnifica Humanitas appear to have been written by AI. Analyses have determined that parts of the Magnifica Humanitas appear to have been written by AI. by Jay Peters and Hayden Field May 27, 2026, 12:38 AM UTC Getty Images It’s possible that AI was used... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: When Pope Leo XIV presented his first encyclical on artificial intelligence at the Vatican on Monday, he invited Christopher Olah, cofounder of Anthropic, to speak. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.
